message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 3/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"For normal-hearing listeners, the dominant acoustic modification driving the clear-speech intelligibility gain in fricatives is a shift of spectral energy toward higher frequency regions, evidenced by significant positive correlations between intelligibility advantage and DFT/LPC spectral-peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1)."

**Source:** https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2007/maniwa07_interspeech.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1)... These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: What does this paper say about clear speech intelligibility of fricatives for normal-hearing listeners? Specifically: (1) Did they find significant positive correlations between intelligibility advantage and DFT/LPC spectral peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1)? (2) Did they conclude that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions is most closely related to overall intelligibility enhancement? (3) What were the listeners (normal-hearing vs hearing-impaired)? Quote the exact relevant findings and any caveats or qualifications.

input.query: Maniwa Jongman Wade 2007 clear speech fricatives intelligibility spectral moment M1 peak location

PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10540850/"},{"title":"Acoustic characteristics of clearly spoken English fricatives","url":"https://saidnazulfiqar.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2009/03/characteristic-spoken-english.pdf"},{"title":"Acoustic classification of Russian plain and palatalized sibilant fricatives: Spectral vs. cepstral measures - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639317303680"}]

Based on the search results, I found information related to your query about Maniwa, Jongman, and Wade's work on fricative acoustic characteristics.

Maniwa, Jongman, and Wade (2007) conducted research on "Acoustic and perceptual properties of clearly produced fricatives", though this appears to have been unpublished dissertation work at that time.

The search results indicate that Maniwa, Jongman, and Wade published their work in 2009 in the Journal of the Acoustical Society of America as "Acoustic characteristics of clearly spoken English fricatives." This later publication appears to be the primary peer-reviewed version of their research.

Regarding the acoustic measures examined in their work: The results indicate that spectral peak location, spectral moments, and both normalized and relative amplitude serve to distinguish all four places of fricative articulation. More specifically, higher spectral peaks, higher spectral centers of gravity (COG), and lower skewness were most strongly correlated with improved intelligibility of English fricatives.

Additionally, the first spectral moment (M1; spectral center of gravity) represents the mean of the energy distribution, and spectral peak location (P) is defined as the frequency that corresponds to the highest amplitude peak of the FFT spectrum.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I now have the exact text of the primary source. Let me read the key passages in full to confirm the precise wording and context.

input.description: Read abstract conclusion and Experiment 1 and 2 results sections

content.content:
benefited both groups overall; however, for impaired
listeners, the clear speech effect held only for sibilant
pairs. Correlation analyses comparing acoustic and
perceptual
data
indicated
that
a
shift
of
energy concentration toward higher frequency regions
and greater source strength contributed to the clear
speech effect for normal-hearing listeners, while listeners
with simulated loss seemed to benefit mostly from cues
involving lower frequency regions.

for sibilants than for non-sibilants. The main effect of
Distinction was also significant (p<.001), with lower
thresholds for voicing distinctions relative to place of
articulation distinctions. A Style × Sibilance interaction
(p<.01) showed that, while both sibilants and nonsibilants were more intelligible in clear speech, the effect
was larger for sibilant pairs. The Style × Distinction
interaction was not significant [F<1]; clear speech
resulted in similar benefits for place and voicing
distinctions.

Figure 1: Speech-to-noise (SNR) thresholds (dB) as a
function of style and fricative pair in Experiment I
Overall, spectral measures appeared to be the
important predictors for improved intelligibility.
Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained
between intelligibility advantages and acoustic
modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral
moment 1 (M1), whereas significant negative correlations
were found for M3. These results suggest that a shift of
spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear
fricatives is most closely related to the overall
intelligibility enhancement.

3. Experiment 2
Experiment 1 results showed that normal hearing
listeners benefit from modifications in clear speech
fricative productions specifically involving a shift in
acoustic energy concentrations toward higher frequency
regions. Since this type of modification may be
problematic for hearing-impaired listeners, we attempted
to replicate the results of the experiment with simulated
hearing impairment.

2.4 Results and discussion

3.1 Simulation
408

 Overall, acoustic-to-intelligibility correlations were
much less consistent than for Experiment I; in particular,
conspicuously absent were the positive correlations with
several spectral measures indicating shifts to higher
frequency regions that were seen for place contrasts in
Exp. I. Since the perception of sibilant and non-sibilant
pairs seemed to be affected differentially by the
impairment, a final set of correlation analyses compared
intelligibility differences across speakers with acoustic
differences separately for each class of sounds. While this
comparison was considerably less well powered than the
others described above, the results were potentially
interesting. For sibilant pairs, marginal positive
correlations (p<0.1) were seen between intelligibility
advantages and M3 and M4, and negative correlations for
DFT and LPC peaks, and M2. For non-sibilant pairs,
correlations were weaker and less straightforward, with
only one significant positive correlate for f0 following the
fricative and marginal correlate for M1, and negative
correlates for F2, RMS amplitude and LPC peak. Thus,
results tended in all cases in the opposite direction of
Experiment 1; the more clear speech modifications
involved higher-frequency acoustic information, the less
effective they were for (simulated) hearing impaired
listeners.
In sum, overall perception results and correlation
analyses for this subject group suggest that, since
voiceless non-sibilants are characterized by the highest
peak values and F2 with diffuse spread of energy below
10 kHz, important spectral cues are less audible/available
to listeners with sloping hearing loss the higher they are
transposed. Sibilants, on the other hand, have both higher
inherent consonant-to-vowel ratios (CVRs) and more
potential cues (esp. palato-alveolar peak frequencies)
involving energy in lower regions. These cues were
generally better-preserved in stimuli with simulated
sloping, recruiting losses, perhaps especially when clear
speech involved more energy at lower frequencies.

techniques used in plasticity-based auditory training tasks,
prosthetic hearing devices, or for speech intelligibility
enhancement. Specifically, our results suggest that, for
normal-hearing listeners, intelligibility benefits can be
readily achieved by transposing important cues to higher
frequency regions. For hearing-impaired listeners,
however, effective alterations may be generally in the
opposite direction and are probably more fricativedependent. It should be noted that these results, based on
simulated elevated thresholds and loudness recruitment,
may not hold for actual hearing impaired listeners or even
for other simulated aspects of impairment, such as
temporal resolution and spectral sensitivity.

content.text:
I have full confirmation directly from the primary source. Let me verify the key elements of the claim against the exact text.

The claim states: "For normal-hearing listeners, the dominant acoustic modification driving the clear-speech intelligibility gain in fricatives is a shift of spectral energy toward higher frequency regions, evidenced by significant positive correlations between intelligibility advantage and DFT/LPC spectral-peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1)."

The paper (Experiment 1, normal-hearing listeners) states verbatim:
- "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1), whereas significant negative correlations were found for M3."
- "These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement."
- Abstract: "a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners."

The claim is precisely and explicitly supported by the primary source — same statistic, same measures, same p-value, same conclusion, and correctly scoped to normal-hearing listeners (Experiment 1). The source is a peer-reviewed conference paper (InterSpeech 2007) and matches the cited URL exactly. Let me do one check for whether the broader literature contradicts or heavily qualifies this.

input.query: clear speech fricatives intelligibility spectral peak higher frequency center of gravity correlation later studies

content.content:
Web search results for query: "clear speech fricatives intelligibility spectral peak higher frequency center of gravity correlation later studies"

Links: [{"title":"Speech perception — Grokipedia","url":"https://grokipedia.com/page/Speech_perception"},{"title":"Representations of fricatives in subcortical model responses: Comparisons with human consonant perception - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10550336/"},{"title":"Acoustic characteristics of fricatives, amplitude of formants and clarity of speech produced without and with a medical mask - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9305964/"},{"title":"Acoustic characteristics of fricatives, amplitude of formants and clarity of speech produced without and with a medical mask - Nguyen - 2022 - International Journal of Language & Communication Disorders - Wiley Online Library","url":"https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/1460-6984.12705"},{"title":"(PDF) Acoustic characteristics of clearly spoken English fricatives","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/26277657_Acoustic_characteristics_of_clearly_spoken_English_fricatives"},{"title":"Phonetics of Fricatives Allard Jongman Please cite as","url":"https://kuppl.ku.edu/sites/kuppl/files/documents/publications/Jongman%20OREL%202024%20Phonetics%20of%20Fricatives.pdf"},{"title":"Refining and extending measures for fricative spectra, with special attention to the high-frequency range - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10540850/"},{"title":"Beyond spectral moments: Validating alternative measures of sibilant fricatives using listener ratings of children's speech | JASA Express Letters | AIP Publishing","url":"https://pubs.aip.org/asa/jel/article/5/10/105202/3365614/Beyond-spectral-moments-Validating-alternative"},{"title":"Beyond spectral moments: Validating alternative measures of sibilant fricatives using listener ratings of children's speech","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC12499953/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what recent research shows about clear speech fricatives and the relationship between spectral characteristics and intelligibility:

## Key Spectral Measures for Fricatives

The most common ways used to quantify fricatives are the skewness, center of gravity, and dispersion of their acoustic spectra. Spectral differences, including peak location and moments like center of gravity, enable reliable perceptual separation, as shown in analyses of English fricatives.

## Spectral Peak and Higher Frequency Characteristics

For sibilant fricatives, /s/ features high-frequency energy concentrated above 4 kHz (spectral peak around 4-8 kHz), contrasting with /ʃ/, which has lower-frequency energy peaking around 2-4 kHz due to a more posterior constriction.

## Clear Speech and Intelligibility Correlation

Previous research has shown that spectral moments of fricatives are important correlates of clear spoken speech. Recent evidence suggests that acoustic information in the higher-frequency regions affects speech intelligibility.

## Recent Studies on Alternative Measures

Later studies have questioned traditional spectral moment measures. The spectral peak in mid-frequency (FM) is proposed as a superior measure of place of articulation of sibilant fricatives to the most commonly used measure, the first spectral moment (M1). Regression models reveal that FM in 3–9 kHz range best predicts listener's ratings of children's fricatives.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
The broader literature is consistent with and corroborates the claim. The companion peer-reviewed JASA paper (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade, 2009) and subsequent fricative-acoustics research support that higher-frequency spectral energy (higher peak location, higher M1/center of gravity) relates to clear-speech fricative intelligibility. While later work (e.g., "Beyond spectral moments") proposes refined measures like mid-frequency peak (FM) as better predictors than M1 for place of articulation, this does not contradict the 2007 finding about the direction of energy shift driving intelligibility — it merely refines the measurement methodology. The claim is correctly scoped to normal-hearing listeners (Experiment 1), which is critical because the paper explicitly found the opposite for simulated hearing-impaired listeners.

The claim is well-supported, correctly scoped, accurately quoted, current in the sense that nothing contradicts it, and backed by the exact primary peer-reviewed source. I cannot refute it.

input.evidence:
VERIFIED AGAINST PRIMARY SOURCE (extracted full text from the actual InterSpeech 2007 PDF: Maniwa, Jongman & Wade, "Acoustic correlates of intelligibility enhancements in clearly produced fricatives"). The claim reproduces the paper's findings exactly, with no overreach:

1. Quote accuracy: The paper's Experiment 1 (normal-hearing listeners) results state verbatim: "Significant positive correlations (p<0.05) were obtained between intelligibility advantages and acoustic modifications in DFT and LPC peak location and spectral moment 1 (M1), whereas significant negative correlations were found for M3. These results suggest that a shift of spectral energy to higher frequency regions in clear fricatives is most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement." The supporting quote is genuine and complete.

2. Correct scoping (this is the critical nuance the claim gets RIGHT): These positive-correlation results are from Experiment 1, which used "14 normal-hearing American English listeners." The abstract confirms: "a shift of energy concentration toward higher frequency regions and greater source strength contributed to the clear speech effect for normal-hearing listeners." The claim correctly restricts to normal-hearing listeners. Critically, the paper found the OPPOSITE for simulated hearing-impaired listeners (Exp. 2): "results tended in all cases in the opposite direction of Experiment 1... For sibilant pairs... negative correlations for DFT and LPC peaks." The claim does not improperly generalize the higher-frequency conclusion to all listeners.

3. "Dominant acoustic modification" phrasing is supported: paper says spectral measures "appeared to be the important predictors for improved intelligibility" and the higher-frequency shift is "most closely related to the overall intelligibility enhancement."

4. Source quality: Peer-reviewed InterSpeech 2007 conference paper; URL matches exactly; corroborated by the authors' fuller peer-reviewed JASA 2009 paper ("Acoustic characteristics of clearly spoken English fricatives") and consistent with subsequent fricative-acoustics literature (higher spectral peak / center-of-gravity linked to fricative intelligibility). Later work ("Beyond spectral moments," 2024) proposes refined measures (mid-frequency peak FM) as better than M1 for place-of-articulation prediction, but this refines measurement methodology — it does not contradict the 2007 direction-of-energy-shift finding for clear-speech intelligibility. No credible source disputes the core claim.

CONFIDENCE: high — verified against the exact primary-source text, statistics (p<0.05), measures (DFT/LPC peak, M1, M3), and listener population all match precisely; the claim is accurately scoped and corroborated by later literature.

content.text: I verified the claim against the actual primary source by extracting the full text of the InterSpeech 2007 PDF (Maniwa, Jongman & Wade) that WebFetch had saved locally. The claim is accurately supported and correctly scoped to normal-hearing listeners, with the exact statistics, measures, and conclusions matching the paper verbatim. The claim is NOT refuted (refuted=false, high confidence).
